He died in 1931. Hoosen Haffejee, the fourth son of Hoosen Natal. He Khatib, was born at Ixopo in 1899. He moved to Durban and established a light furniture factory in 1914 in the central area. He was a pioneer smgnast the early Muslims in the furniture field. He later moved to Kilkenny Road, Mayville and in 1954, to Bellair Road, Cato Manor. Expropriation, however, had a considerable effect on further expansion and the factory continues under Council tenancy to this day, until new premises are built in the near future in the Isipingo industrial complex. In 1948, Ahmed Haffejee, the eldest son of Mahmood Hoosen Haffejee, joined his father in business and by 1958 had added a new division to the factory. The manufacturing of Kitchen furniture was undertaken and the expansion has continued | ! to such an extent that the Haffejee Furniture Manufacturers is now acknowledged amongst the top 5 in the Republic in this field. title_or_profession: The grand old lady of Rustenburg whose story of the carly days of Indian settlement in the Western Transvaal reads like a fairy tale, died in August, 1971, at the age of hundred and twelve
biography: The grand old lady of Rustenburg whose story of the carly days of Indian settlement in the Western Transvaal reads like a fairy tale, died in August, 1971, at the age of hundred and twelve. She came to this country during the days of the ox-waggon and made Rustenburg her home. Her husband, one of the leading merchants of the pre-Boer War era, was a friend and contemporary of Paul Kruger. He was so esteemed by the President that, over the years, the duplicate key of the Bank of Rustenburg was entrusted to his care. During the Boer war it was the Bhayat family which provided over £11,000 worth of much needed saddles and saddlery to the Boer armed forces. Bhayat was away in India at the time but his cabfe in the affirmative to Paul Kruger’s request was eloquent of their mutual esteem and regard. Fathima Bhayat was a remarkable woman and a true-blue pioneer. He eyesight was un-dimmed by time. She read the Koran to the end of her days, unwilling and finding it uncessary to use glasses. Her hearing was good and her enthusiasm to relate and relive those colourful days in the out-back was boundless and so was her capacity to recall each precious incident and personal experience. Her death in 1971, cut off the last remaining link with Rustenburg’s early past. kloof Trading Stores at Isinenbe. The family resides at Isinenbe; Bus. Add.: P. O. ABOOBAKER, Hajee 11492, Ahmed Merchant class to settle here, Jhavery: The first of the Aboobaker Shavery arrived in this country in 1872 with a ship-load of Indian spices from Mauritius. This was in response to requests by indentured immigrants for Indian goods and the Natal government approach to the Mauritian authorities, He had an Import-Export business in Calcutta and was the first to export dry fish to Mauritius for this country. He was also said to be a ship-owner besides being a charterer of vessels. In those days the me! nt class were considered as Arabs due no doubt to their religious links, He was a shrewd businessman and was the first Indian to own land and property in the Transvaal. He was one of the biggest importers of silks, cottons, spices, ghee, rice and condi- ments in those early days. Public spirited and religiously inclined, it was he who donated the first site towards a Muslim place of worship in Durban. The Juma Masjid in Grey Street stands on the very site donated by Aboobaker Jhavery. He contributed generously to the community's advance in those carly years. On one of his trips to India he contacted Cholera in Bombay and succombed to the disease in 1887 at the age of thirty seven. Aboobaker Shavery was the fore-runner to the thousands of Merchant class Indians to arrive in this country. Bey JHAVERI, na3 Hajee Ismail Aboo- baker: b. 1878, Durban, Natal.
